tools.description: Create a new GitHub issue for tracking bugs, feature requests, or tasks. Use this for actionable work items that need assignment, labeling, and status tracking. For reports, announcements, or status updates that don't require task tracking, use create_discussion instead. CONSTRAINTS: Maximum 1 issue(s) can be created.

body.description: Detailed issue description in Markdown. Do NOT repeat the title as a heading since it already appears as the issue's h1. Include context, reproduction steps, or acceptance criteria as appropriate.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

labels.description: Labels to categorize the issue (e.g., 'bug', 'enhancement'). Labels must exist in the repository.

parent.description: Parent issue number for creating sub-issues. This is the numeric ID from the GitHub URL (e.g., 42 in github.com/owner/repo/issues/42). Can also be a temporary_id (e.g., 'aw_abc123', 'aw_Test123') from a previously created issue in the same workflow run.

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

temporary_id.description: Unique temporary identifier for referencing this issue before it's created. Format: 'aw_' followed by 3 to 12 alphanumeric characters (e.g., 'aw_abc1', 'aw_Test123'). Use '#aw_ID' in body text to reference other issues by their temporary_id; these are replaced with actual issue numbers after creation.

title.description: Concise issue title summarizing the bug, feature, or task. The title appears as the main heading, so keep it brief and descriptive.

tools.name: create_issue

tools.description: Report that a tool or capability needed to complete the task is not available, or share any information you deem important about missing functionality or limitations. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because the required functionality is missing or access is restricted.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this tool is needed or what information you want to share about the limitation (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tool.description: Optional: Name or description of the missing tool or capability (max 128 characters). Be specific about what functionality is needed.

tools.name: missing_tool

tools.description: Log a transparency message when no significant actions are needed. Use this to confirm workflow completion and provide visibility when analysis is complete but no changes or outputs are required (e.g., 'No issues found', 'All checks passed'). This ensures the workflow produces human-visible output even when no other actions are taken.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

message.description: Status or completion message to log. Should explain what was analyzed and the outcome (e.g., 'Code review complete - no issues found', 'Analysis complete - all tests passing').

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: noop

tools.description: Report that data or information needed to complete the task is not available. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because required data, context, or information is missing.

alternatives.description: Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).

context.description: Additional context about the missing data or where it should come from (max 256 characters).

data_type.description: Type or description of the missing data or information (max 128 characters). Be specific about what data is needed.

integrity.description: Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., "low", "medium", "high").

reason.description: Explanation of why this data is needed to complete the task (max 256 characters).

secrecy.description: Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., "public", "internal", "private").

tools.name: missing_data

tools.description: Signal that the task could not be completed due to an infrastructure or tool failure (e.g., MCP server crash, missing authentication, inaccessible repository). Use this when required tools or data are unavailable and the task cannot be meaningfully performed. This is distinct from noop (no action needed) — it indicates an active failure that prevented the task from running. The workflow framework will treat this as a failure signal even when the agent exits successfully.

details.description: Optional extended details or diagnostic context about the failure (max 65000 characters).

reason.description: A concise explanation of why the task could not be completed (max 1024 characters). Be specific about which tools or resources were unavailable.

tools.name: report_incomplete

tools.description: Dispatch the issue triage workflow for the newly created issue
tools.name: dispatch_triage

tools.description: Get details for a commit from a GitHub repository

include_diff.description: Whether to include file diffs and stats in the response. Default is true.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

sha.description: Commit SHA, branch name, or tag name

tools.name: get_commit

tools.description: Get the contents of a file or directory from a GitHub repository

ref.description: Accepts optional git refs such as `refs/tags/{tag}`, `refs/heads/{branch}` or `refs/pull/{pr_number}/head`

sha.description: Accepts optional commit SHA. If specified, it will be used instead of ref

tools.name: get_file_contents

annotations.title: Get a specific label from a repository.

tools.description: Get a specific label from a repository.
tools.name: get_label

tools.description: Get the latest release in a GitHub repository
tools.name: get_latest_release

annotations.title: Get a release by tag name

tools.description: Get a specific release by its tag name in a GitHub repository
tools.name: get_release_by_tag

tools.description: Get details about a specific git tag in a GitHub repository
tools.name: get_tag

tools.description: Get information about a specific issue in a GitHub repository.

method.description:
The read operation to perform on a single issue.
Options are:
1. get - Get details of a specific issue.
2. get_comments - Get issue comments.
3. get_sub_issues - Get sub-issues of the issue.
4. get_labels - Get labels assigned to the issue.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: issue_read

tools.description: List branches in a GitHub repository

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: list_branches

tools.description: Get list of commits of a branch in a GitHub repository. Returns at least 30 results per page by default, but can return more if specified using the perPage parameter (up to 100).

author.description: Author username or email address to filter commits by

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

sha.description: Commit SHA, branch or tag name to list commits of. If not provided, uses the default branch of the repository. If a commit SHA is provided, will list commits up to that SHA.

tools.name: list_commits

tools.description: List supported issue types for repository owner (organization).

owner.description: The organization owner of the repository

tools.name: list_issue_types

tools.description: List issues in a GitHub repository. For pagination, use the 'endCursor' from the previous response's 'pageInfo' in the 'after' parameter.

after.description: Cursor for pagination. Use the endCursor from the previous page's PageInfo for GraphQL APIs.

direction.description: Order direction. If provided, the 'orderBy' also needs to be provided.

orderBy.description: Order issues by field. If provided, the 'direction' also needs to be provided.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

state.description: Filter by state, by default both open and closed issues are returned when not provided

tools.name: list_issues

tools.description: List releases in a GitHub repository

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: list_releases

tools.description: List git tags in a GitHub repository

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

tools.name: list_tags

tools.description: Fast and precise code search across ALL GitHub repositories using GitHub's native search engine. Best for finding exact symbols, functions, classes, or specific code patterns.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Search query using GitHub's powerful code search syntax. Examples: 'content:Skill language:Java org:github', 'NOT is:archived language:Python OR language:go', 'repo:github/github-mcp-server'. Supports exact matching, language filters, path filters, and more.

tools.name: search_code

tools.description: Search for issues in GitHub repositories using issues search syntax already scoped to is:issue

owner.description: Optional repository owner. If provided with repo, only issues for this repository are listed.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Search query using GitHub issues search syntax

repo.description: Optional repository name. If provided with owner, only issues for this repository are listed.

sort.description: Sort field by number of matches of categories, defaults to best match

tools.name: search_issues

tools.description: Find GitHub repositories by name, description, readme, topics, or other metadata. Perfect for discovering projects, finding examples, or locating specific repositories across GitHub.

minimal_output.description: Return minimal repository information (default: true). When false, returns full GitHub API repository objects.

perPage.description: Results per page for pagination (min 1, max 100)

query.description: Repository search query. Examples: 'machine learning in:name stars:>1000 language:python', 'topic:react', 'user:facebook'. Supports advanced search syntax for precise filtering.

sort.description: Sort repositories by field, defaults to best match

tools.name: search_repositories

Co-authored-by: copilot-swe-agent[bot] <198982749+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>","author":{"name":"Jesse Squire","email":"jesse.squire@gmail.com","date":"2026-04-20T23:07:32Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-20T23:07:32Z"}},"author":{"login":"jsquire","id":913445,"profile_url":"https://github.com/jsquire","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/913445?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"[REDACTED]","html_url":"https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-net/commit/[REDACTED]","commit":{"message":"Remove duplicate top-level `required` on credential definition (#58408)

Both Azure.Core and System.ClientModel ConfigurationSchema.json segments
declared `required: ["CredentialSource"]` at the top of
`definitions/credential`. When the appsettings schema combiner merges
segments by definition name, it concatenates `required` arrays without
dedup, producing `["CredentialSource", "CredentialSource"]` in the
consumer's combined AppSettingsSchema.json. System.ClientModel already
requires CredentialSource, so removing it from Azure.Core's top-level
credential definition eliminates the duplicate while preserving all
`if`-clause requireds and the chainableCredential definition.

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>","author":{"name":"m-nash","email":"64171366+m-nash@users.noreply.github.com","date":"2026-04-20T22:37:32Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-04-20T22:37:32Z"}},"author":{"login":"m-nash","id":64171366,"profile_url":"https://github.com/m-nash","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/64171366?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}}]

# Azure SDK for .NET Library Inventory Guide

This document outlines how to generate and maintain the inventory of libraries in the Azure SDK for .NET repository.

## Purpose

The library inventory helps us:

1. Track all Azure.* libraries in the repository (legacy non-Azure.* libraries are excluded)
2. Categorize them as data plane or management plane
3. Identify which generator (New Emitter, Autorest, or Old TypeSpec) they use
4. Plan migrations to newer generators

## Generating the Inventory

To generate the library inventory:

1. Run the `Library_Inventory.ps1` script from the repository root:

```powershell
pwsh doc/GeneratorMigration/Library_Inventory.ps1
```

2. The script will:
   - Scan all libraries in the `sdk/` directory
   - Filter out libraries that don't start with "Azure.*" (legacy libraries)
   - Categorize them by type (data plane or management plane)
   - Identify the generator used (New Emitter, Autorest, or Old TypeSpec)
   - Generate a markdown report (`doc/GeneratorMigration/Library_Inventory.md`)

3. If you need JSON output for programmatic use, use the `-Json` flag:

```powershell
pwsh doc/GeneratorMigration/Library_Inventory.ps1 -Json
```
   - This will generate an additional `Library_Inventory.json` file with the raw inventory data
   - Note: The JSON file is for temporary analytical use and should not be checked into the repository

## Inventory Classification Logic

The script uses the following logic to categorize libraries:

### Data Plane vs Management Plane

- **Management Plane**: Libraries that contain "Azure.ResourceManager" or ".Management." in their path
- **Data Plane**: All other libraries

### Swagger vs TypeSpec (TSP)

The script looks for evidence of TypeSpec usage in the following order:

1. Direct TypeSpec indicators:
   - `tsp-location.yaml` file with an `emitterPackageJsonPath` value
   - `src/tspconfig.yaml` file
   - `src/tsp` directory
   - `.tsp` files in the src directory

2. Swagger indicators:
   - Presence of `src/autorest.md` file
   - `src/Generated` directory exists
   - Files with `<auto-generated/>` comment headers

If none of these TypeSpec indicators are found but there is evidence of code generation 
(like a `src/Generated` directory or files with `<auto-generated/>` markers), the script assumes Swagger.

## Understanding the Results

The inventory markdown file provides:

1. **Table of Contents**: Quick navigation links to all sections
2. **Overall summary counts** with breakdown by generator type
3. Two main tables for libraries with TypeSpec (tsp-location.yaml):
   - **Data Plane Libraries (DPG) - Migrated to New Emitter**: Client APIs using TypeSpec
     - Columns: Service, Library, New Emitter
     - Migration status shows: migrated / total TypeSpec libraries (e.g., "16 / 32 (50%)")
     - **Only includes libraries with tsp-location.yaml file**
     - Shows ✅ for libraries using new emitters, blank for old TypeSpec
   - **Management Plane Libraries (MPG) - Migrated to New Emitter**: Resource management APIs using TypeSpec
     - Columns: Service, Library, New Emitter
     - Migration status shows: migrated / total TypeSpec libraries (e.g., "24 / 66 (36.4%)")
     - **Only includes libraries with tsp-location.yaml file**
     - Shows ✅ for libraries using new emitters, blank for old TypeSpec
4. Two separate tables for libraries still on Swagger:
   - **Data Plane Libraries (DPG) - Still on Swagger**: Libraries not yet migrated to TypeSpec
     - Columns: Service, Library
   - **Management Plane Libraries (MPG) - Still on Swagger**: Libraries not yet migrated to TypeSpec
     - Columns: Service, Library
5. Libraries with no generator (listed separately)

The migration tables use ✅ emoji to indicate the generator type:
- **New Emitter** column: ✅ for libraries using new TypeSpec emitters (@azure-typespec/http-client-csharp, @azure-typespec/http-client-csharp-mgmt, or @typespec/http-client-csharp)
- **No checkmark**: Libraries using old TypeSpec (TSP-Old) with tsp-location.yaml but not yet migrated to new emitter

**Important**: 
- Migration tables only include libraries that have a `tsp-location.yaml` file. Libraries without this file are not included in the migration tracking tables.
- Migration status percentages are calculated as: (libraries with new emitter) / (total libraries with tsp-location.yaml in that table) × 100

Libraries still on Swagger/Autorest are listed in separate "Still on Swagger" tables and are not included in the migration tables.

The migration status shows the number of libraries migrated to the new emitter out of the total, along with the percentage.

## Updating the Inventory

The inventory should be updated whenever:

1. New libraries are added to the repository
2. Libraries are migrated from Swagger to TypeSpec
3. Preparing for migration planning

Simply run the script again to regenerate the inventory files.

arguments.body:
## Documentation Gap **Package:** `doc/GeneratorMigration` **Service directory:** `doc/GeneratorMigration/` **Triggered by:** PR #58372 (commit f288cfe) by @ArthurMa1978 ## What Changed PR #58372 updated `Library_Inventory.ps1` to add: 1. A **`Using SCM`** column in the Data Plane Libraries (DPG) migrated-to-new-emitter table — marks libraries using `@typespec/http-client-csharp` (System.ClientModel) with ✅ 2. A new **Provisioning Libraries** section in the generated report, with columns for `Mgmt Peer Library` and `Generator` (Reflection / TypeSpec / None), plus a migration status line 3. New **scan progress reporting** (`Write-Progress` + `Write-Host` messages) emitted while the script scans all service directories However, the companion document `Library_Inventory_Guide.md` was **not updated** and still describes the old output format. ## Gaps Found - `Library_Inventory_Guide.md` "Understanding the Results" section describes only two columns (`Service | Library | New Emitter`) for the DPG migrated table, but the output now has four columns (`Service | Library | New Emitter | Using SCM`) - The `Using SCM` column is not explained anywhere in the guide (what it means, which emitter package triggers it, how to interpret ✅ vs blank) - The guide does not mention the **Provisioning Libraries** section at all — neither the table structure (`Service | Library | Mgmt Peer Library | Generator`), the generator values (`Reflection`, `TypeSpec`, `None`), nor the migration status line - The guide does not mention the scan progress output printed to the console while the script runs - The summary statistics section in the guide does not reflect the new Provisioning breakdown (`Reflection-based generator`, `TypeSpec-based generator`, `No generator`)
📐 Implementation Guide This section contains step-by-step instructions for a coding agent to implement the changes described above. ### Step 1: Modify files **File:** `doc/GeneratorMigration/Library_Inventory_Guide.md` **Action:** Edit — update the "Understanding the Results" section #### 1a. Update the Data Plane Migrated table description Locate and replace the block that describes the DPG migrated table columns: ```markdown - **Data Plane Libraries (DPG) - Migrated to New Emitter**: Client APIs using TypeSpec - Columns: Service, Library, New Emitter - Migration status shows: migrated / total TypeSpec libraries (e.g., "16 / 32 (50%)") - **Only includes libraries with tsp-location.yaml file** - Shows ✅ for libraries using new emitters, blank for old TypeSpec ``` Replace with: ```markdown - **Data Plane Libraries (DPG) - Migrated to New Emitter**: Client APIs using TypeSpec - Columns: Service, Library, New Emitter, Using SCM - Migration status shows: migrated / total TypeSpec libraries (e.g., "16 / 32 (50%)") - **Only includes libraries with tsp-location.yaml file** - Shows ✅ in **New Emitter** for libraries using new TypeSpec emitters; blank for old TypeSpec - Shows ✅ in **Using SCM** for libraries whose `tsp-location.yaml` references the `@typespec/http-client-csharp` emitter — these libraries are built on `System.ClientModel` rather than `Azure.Core`; blank otherwise ``` #### 1b. Add a Provisioning Libraries entry to the results section After the "5. Libraries with no generator" bullet (currently the last bullet), add: ```markdown 6. **Provisioning Libraries**: Infrastructure-as-code libraries built on the `Azure.Provisioning.*` pattern - Columns: Service, Library, Mgmt Peer Library, Generator - **Mgmt Peer Library**: The management-plane (`Azure.ResourceManager.*`) package that the provisioning library wraps. Libraries whose peer has been migrated to the new emitter display ✅ next to the peer name - **Generator** values: - `TypeSpec ✅` — library is generated from TypeSpec using the `@azure-typespec/http-client-csharp-provisioning` emitter - `Reflection` — library uses the legacy reflection-based provisioning generator - `None` — library has no code generator - Migration status shows: TypeSpec-based count / total provisioning libraries (e.g., "3 / 40 migrated to TypeSpec-based generator") ``` #### 1c. Update the legend / column-description block near the bottom Locate the existing legend block: ```markdown The migration tables use ✅ emoji to indicate the generator type: - **New Emitter** column: ✅ for libraries using new TypeSpec emitters (@azure-typespec/http-client-csharp, @azure-typespec/http-client-csharp-mgmt, or @typespec/http-client-csharp) - **No checkmark**: Libraries using old TypeSpec (TSP-Old) with tsp-location.yaml but not yet migrated to new emitter ``` Replace with: ```markdown The migration tables use ✅ emoji to indicate status: - **New Emitter** column: ✅ for libraries using new TypeSpec emitters (`@azure-typespec/http-client-csharp`, `@azure-typespec/http-client-csharp-mgmt`, or `@typespec/http-client-csharp`); blank for old TypeSpec (TSP-Old) - **Using SCM** column (Data Plane table only): ✅ for libraries built on `System.ClientModel` (`@typespec/http-client-csharp` emitter); blank for libraries built on `Azure.Core` - **Generator** column (Provisioning table): `TypeSpec ✅`, `Reflection`, or `None` indicating the generator used for each provisioning library - **Mgmt Peer Library** column (Provisioning table): the corresponding `Azure.ResourceManager.*` package; ✅ next to the name means that peer has been migrated to the new TypeSpec emitter ``` #### 1d. Add a note about scan progress output After the existing "Usage" step 2 block (which lists what the script does), add: ```markdown - Prints scan progress to the console in 10% increments (e.g., `Scan progress: 30% (45/150 services)`) and uses PowerShell's `Write-Progress` for a progress bar in supporting terminals ``` ### Step 2: Add or update code snippets No code snippets are needed — this is a pure prose documentation update. ### Step 3: Verify README structure `Library_Inventory_Guide.md` is an internal tooling guide, not a published SDK README. Standard SDK README sections do not apply here. Verify only that: - All four new items above are present and accurate - The guide still reads coherently from top to bottom after the edits ### Step 4: Validate Run these commands in order: 1. Run the script to regenerate the inventory and confirm its output matches the guide's description: ```powershell pwsh doc/GeneratorMigration/Library_Inventory.ps1 ``` 2. Visually diff `doc/GeneratorMigration/Library_Inventory.md` against the descriptions in the updated guide to confirm accuracy
## Next Steps > [!TIP] > **Ready for automated implementation?** Assign this issue to **@copilot** to have Copilot coding agent implement the changes described in the Implementation Guide above
arguments.title: [GeneratorMigration] Docs: Update Library_Inventory_Guide.md to document new SCM column, Provisioning section, and scan progress
